ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class; no specific lotus paste pau entry exists. FOODON_03315091 used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers steamed bread and buns broadly; no lotus-paste-filled variant code exists. Used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lotus paste pau or filled steamed bun as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium lotus paste pau weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single serving at breakfast or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Lotus Paste Pau — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Lotus Paste Pau (cr_id F05020062). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central steamed bun analogue as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Steamed bun (Chinese, plain) — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for lotus paste pau. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 65 for plain Chinese steamed buns (mantou/baozi). Lotus paste pau has a similar wheat-flour dough base but a higher sugar content from the sweetened filling, which may elevate the effective GI slightly. GI 65 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (41.9 g for 80 g serving) / 100 = 27.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.4 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 81 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low (81 mg/100 g) and is not the primary concern; however, the high sugar load (23.4 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction per AHA 2018 guidance, so individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one small pau occasionally and prefer plain steamed buns (mantou) as a lower-sugar alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (23.4 g/100 g) and high refined carbohydrate (51.9 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g), causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lotus paste pau is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content (23.4 g/100 g, exceeding the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling) and high refined carbohydrate load (51.9 g/100 g) with minimal fibre buffering (2.3 g/100 g); if consumed, limit to half a pau as an occasional treat and pair with a protein source to partially attenuate the glycaemic spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g estimated) and high added sugar (23.4 g/100 g) contributing to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high added sugar content (23.4 g/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) in lotus paste pau may contribute to elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and choose wholegrain or lower-sugar alternatives to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lotus paste pau (莲蓉包, Lian Rong Bao) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese steamed bun filled with sweetened lotus seed paste. The outer dough is made from wheat flour, yeast, sugar, and lard or vegetable shortening, then steamed until soft and fluffy. The filling is a smooth, mildly sweet paste prepared from dried lotus seeds cooked with sugar and oil. The bun has a pillowy, slightly chewy texture with a dense, fragrant, and subtly earthy-sweet filling. It is commonly eaten at breakfast or as a mid-morning snack and is a staple at dim sum restaurants and traditional Chinese bakeries across Singapore. Nutritionally, lotus paste pau is energy-dense at 302 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate content (51.9 g/100 g) dominated by added sugar (23.4 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g). The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a high glycaemic load of approximately 27 per standard 80 g serving. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose, and should be consumed only occasionally by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar and moderate saturated fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lotus paste pau?
Can diabetics eat lotus paste pau?
Is lotus bun suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of lotus paste pau?
How much sugar is in a lotus paste pau?
What is the difference between lotus paste pau and red bean pau?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id F05020062, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for Chinese steamed bun and documented. All mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean cuisine are present.
notes: Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central steamed bun analogue (SR Legacy steamed bun class) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this item. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient, fatty acid subtype, and moisture data for lotus paste pau are not reported in the HPB Foodo Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific filled steamed bun as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g (-1): fatty acid subtype breakdown not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this item; polyunsaturated_fat_g (-1): fatty acid subtype breakdown not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this item; trans_fat_g (-1): trans fat not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this item and no approved source confirms a specific value — the input record's estimated 0.1 g/100 g is not adopted as it lacks approved-source confirmation; potassium_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; calcium_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; iron_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; cholesterol_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_c_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_a_mcg_rae (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_b6_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_b12_mcg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; folate_mcg_dfe (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_d_mcg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; vitamin_e_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; phosphorus_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; magnesium_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; zinc_mg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; selenium_mcg (-1): not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; water_g (-1): moisture content not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this item. Halal status is not confirmed as lard may be used in traditional preparations; consumers requiring halal-certified products should verify with the specific vendor. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on plain Chinese steamed bun from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of lotus paste pau may be higher due to the high sugar filling.
